AI.motivation: Open-world embodied agents struggle with long-horizon tasks where the main bottleneck is not single-step planning quality but how interaction experience is organized and evolved over time. Current approaches need better mechanisms to structure and leverage past experience.
AI.method: The framework has three phases: 1) Experience Anchoring - solidifies subgoal attempts into structured experience tuples with multi-dimensional indexing for efficient recall, 2) Experience Distillation - generalizes successful trajectories into reusable skills and failures into guardrails, 3) Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control - injects retrieved skills/guardrails into LLM planner with diagnosis-triggered replanning.
AI.result: Experiments on the long-horizon suite of Minecraft MCU demonstrate consistent improvements over static-retrieval baselines, showing the effectiveness of the self-evolving framework for organizing interaction experience.
AI.conclusion: Steve-Evolving provides a non-parametric self-evolving framework that enables continual improvement through experience organization and knowledge distillation, offering a promising approach for long-horizon embodied tasks without requiring model parameter updates.

title: Generalist Large Language Models for Molecular Property Prediction: Distilling Knowledge from Specialist Models
summary: Molecular Property Prediction (MPP) is a central task in drug discovery. While Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise as generalist models for MPP, their current performance remains below the threshold for practical adoption. We propose TreeKD, a novel knowledge distillation method that transfers complementary knowledge from tree-based specialist models into LLMs. Our approach trains specialist decision trees on functional group features, then verbalizes their learned predictive rules as natural language to enable rule-augmented context learning. This enables LLMs to leverage structural insights that are difficult to extract from SMILES strings alone. We further introduce rule-consistency, a test-time scaling technique inspired by bagging that ensembles predictions across diverse rules from a Random Forest. Experiments on 22 ADMET properties from the TDC benchmark demonstrate that TreeKD substantially improves LLM performance, narrowing the gap with SOTA specialist models and advancing toward practical generalist models for molecular property prediction.

AI.tldr: TreeKD transfers knowledge from tree-based specialist models to LLMs by verbalizing predictive rules as natural language for rule-augmented context learning in molecular property prediction.
AI.motivation: Current LLM performance in molecular property prediction remains below practical adoption thresholds, despite their promise as generalist models for drug discovery tasks.
AI.method: Train specialist decision trees on functional group features, verbalize their learned predictive rules as natural language for rule-augmented context learning, and use rule-consistency test-time scaling inspired by bagging to ensemble predictions across diverse rules from a Random Forest.
AI.result: Experiments on 22 ADMET properties from TDC benchmark show TreeKD substantially improves LLM performance, narrowing the gap with SOTA specialist models.
AI.conclusion: TreeKD advances toward practical generalist models for molecular property prediction by enabling LLMs to leverage structural insights difficult to extract from SMILES strings alone.

title: When Right Meets Wrong: Bilateral Context Conditioning with Reward-Confidence Correction for GRPO
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as an effective method for training reasoning models. While it computes advantages based on group mean, GRPO treats each output as an independent sample during the optimization and overlooks a vital structural signal: the natural contrast between correct and incorrect solutions within the same group, thus ignoring the rich, comparative data that could be leveraged by explicitly pitting successful reasoning traces against failed ones. To capitalize on this, we present a contrastive reformulation of GRPO, showing that the GRPO objective implicitly maximizes the margin between the policy ratios of correct and incorrect samples. Building on this insight, we propose Bilateral Context Conditioning (BICC), a mechanism that allows the model to cross-reference successful and failed reasoning traces during the optimization, enabling a direct information flow across samples. We further introduce Reward-Confidence Correction (RCC) to stabilize training by dynamically adjusts the advantage baseline in GRPO using reward-confidence covariance derived from the first-order approximation of the variance-minimizing estimator. Both mechanisms require no additional sampling or auxiliary models and can be adapted to all GRPO variants. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements across comprehensive models and algorithms. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Skylanding/BiCC}{https://github.com/Skylanding/BiCC}.

AI.tldr: A contrastive reformulation of GRPO with Bilateral Context Conditioning and Reward-Confidence Correction improves reasoning models by leveraging natural contrasts between correct/incorrect solutions.
AI.motivation: GRPO treats outputs as independent samples during optimization, overlooking the natural contrast between correct and incorrect solutions within the same group, thus ignoring rich comparative data that could improve learning.
AI.method: 1) Shows GRPO implicitly maximizes margin between policy ratios of correct/incorrect samples. 2) Proposes Bilateral Context Conditioning (BICC) to cross-reference successful/failed reasoning traces during optimization. 3) Introduces Reward-Confidence Correction (RCC) to stabilize training by dynamically adjusting advantage baseline using reward-confidence covariance.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements across comprehensive models and algorithms. The method requires no additional sampling or auxiliary models and can adapt to all GRPO variants.
AI.conclusion: The contrastive reformulation of GRPO with BICC and RCC successfully leverages comparative data between correct and incorrect solutions, leading to improved reasoning model performance while maintaining efficiency.

title: Budget-Sensitive Discovery Scoring: A Formally Verified Framework for Evaluating AI-Guided Scientific Selection
summary:
Scientific discovery increasingly relies on AI systems to select candidates for expensive experimental validation, yet no principled, budget-aware evaluation framework exists for comparing selection strategies -- a gap intensified by large language models (LLMs), which generate plausible scientific proposals without reliable downstream evaluation. We introduce the Budget-Sensitive Discovery Score (BSDS), a formally verified metric -- 20 theorems machine-checked by the Lean 4 proof assistant -- that jointly penalizes false discoveries (lambda-weighted FDR) and excessive abstention (gamma-weighted coverage gap) at each budget level. Its budget-averaged form, the Discovery Quality Score (DQS), provides a single summary statistic that no proposer can inflate by performing well at a cherry-picked budget.
  As a case study, we apply BSDS/DQS to: do LLMs add marginal value to an existing ML pipeline for drug discovery candidate selection? We evaluate 39 proposers -- 11 mechanistic variants, 14 zero-shot LLM configurations, and 14 few-shot LLM configurations -- using SMILES representations on MoleculeNet HIV (41,127 compounds, 3.5% active, 1,000 bootstrap replicates) under both random and scaffold splits. Three findings emerge. First, the simple RF-based Greedy-ML proposer achieves the best DQS (-0.046), outperforming all MLP variants and LLM configurations. Second, no LLM surpasses the Greedy-ML baseline under zero-shot or few-shot evaluation on HIV or Tox21, establishing that LLMs provide no marginal value over an existing trained classifier. Third, the proposer hierarchy generalizes across five MoleculeNet benchmarks spanning 0.18%-46.2% prevalence, a non-drug AV safety domain, and a 9x7 grid of penalty parameters (tau >= 0.636, mean tau = 0.863). The framework applies to any setting where candidates are selected under budget constraints and asymmetric error costs.

AI.tldr: A new evaluation framework (BSDS/DQS) for budget-aware scientific discovery selection, tested on drug discovery to show LLMs don't outperform simple ML baselines.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems for scientific discovery lack principled budget-aware evaluation frameworks, especially important with LLMs generating plausible but potentially unreliable scientific proposals without clear evaluation metrics for selection strategies.
AI.method: Introduces Budget-Sensitive Discovery Score (BSDS) - a formally verified metric (20 Lean 4 theorems) that jointly penalizes false discoveries and excessive abstention at each budget level, with its budget-averaged form Discovery Quality Score (DQS). Applied to drug discovery using 39 proposers on MoleculeNet datasets with 1,000 bootstrap replicates.
AI.result: Simple RF-based Greedy-ML achieved best DQS (-0.046), outperforming all MLP variants and LLM configurations. No LLM surpassed Greedy-ML baseline under zero-shot or few-shot evaluation on HIV or Tox21, showing LLMs provide no marginal value over existing trained classifiers. Results generalize across multiple benchmarks and penalty parameters.
AI.conclusion: The BSDS/DQS framework provides a principled way to evaluate candidate selection strategies under budget constraints, demonstrating that LLMs don't add value over simple ML baselines in drug discovery candidate selection, with implications for other budget-constrained scientific discovery settings.

title: Developing and evaluating a chatbot to support maternal health care
comment: 17 pages; submitted to IJCAI 2026 AI and Social Good Track
summary:
The ability to provide trustworthy maternal health information using phone-based chatbots can have a significant impact, particularly in low-resource settings where users have low health literacy and limited access to care. However, deploying such systems is technically challenging: user queries are short, underspecified, and code-mixed across languages, answers require regional context-specific grounding, and partial or missing symptom context makes safe routing decisions difficult.
  We present a chatbot for maternal health in India developed through a partnership between academic researchers, a health tech company, a public health nonprofit, and a hospital. The system combines (1) stage-aware triage, routing high-risk queries to expert templates, (2) hybrid retrieval over curated maternal/newborn guidelines, and (3) evidence-conditioned generation from an LLM. Our core contribution is an evaluation workflow for high-stakes deployment under limited expert supervision. Targeting both component-level and end-to-end testing, we introduce: (i) a labeled triage benchmark (N=150) achieving 86.7% emergency recall, explicitly reporting the missed-emergency vs. over-escalation trade-off; (ii) a synthetic multi-evidence retrieval benchmark (N=100) with chunk-level evidence labels; (iii) LLM-as-judge comparison on real queries (N=781) using clinician-codesigned criteria; and (iv) expert validation. Our findings show that trustworthy medical assistants in multilingual, noisy settings require defense-in-depth design paired with multi-method evaluation, rather than any single model and evaluation method choice.

AI.tldr: A maternal health chatbot for India combines stage-aware triage, hybrid retrieval, and evidence-conditioned LLM generation with a multi-method evaluation workflow for safe deployment in low-resource settings with multilingual, noisy user queries.
AI.motivation: Need for trustworthy maternal health information in low-resource settings where users have low health literacy and limited access to care, but deployment is challenging due to short/underspecified code-mixed queries, need for regional context, and partial symptom information making safe routing difficult.
AI.method: Chatbot developed through multi-stakeholder partnership combines: (1) stage-aware triage routing high-risk queries to expert templates, (2) hybrid retrieval over curated maternal/newborn guidelines, and (3) evidence-conditioned generation from LLM. Evaluation workflow includes component-level and end-to-end testing with triage benchmark, synthetic retrieval benchmark, LLM-as-judge comparison, and expert validation.
AI.result: Developed comprehensive evaluation workflow achieving 86.7% emergency recall on triage benchmark with explicit trade-off reporting, created multi-evidence retrieval benchmark, conducted LLM-as-judge comparison on 781 real queries using clinician-codesigned criteria, and performed expert validation.
AI.conclusion: Trustworthy medical assistants in multilingual, noisy settings require defense-in-depth design (multiple complementary components) paired with multi-method evaluation, rather than relying on any single model or evaluation method choice.

title: Spatial PDE-aware Selective State-space with Nested Memory for Mobile Traffic Grid Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting in cellular networks is a challenging spatiotemporal prediction problem due to strong temporal dependencies, spatial heterogeneity across cells, and the need for scalability to large network deployments. Traditional cell-specific models incur prohibitive training and maintenance costs, while global models often fail to capture heterogeneous spatial dynamics. Recent spatiotemporal architectures based on attention or graph neural networks improve accuracy but introduce high computational overhead, limiting their applicability in large-scale or real-time settings. We study spatiotemporal grid forecasting, where each time step is a 2D lattice of traffic values, and predict the next grid patch using previous patches. We propose NeST-S6, a convolutional selective state-space model (SSM) with a spatial PDE-aware core, implemented in a nested learning paradigm: convolutional local spatial mixing feeds a spatial PDE-aware SSM core, while a nested-learning long-term memory is updated by a learned optimizer when one-step prediction errors indicate unmodeled dynamics. On the mobile-traffic grid (Milan dataset) at three resolutions (202, 502, 1002), NeST-S6 attains lower errors than a strong Mamba-family baseline in both single-step and 6-step autoregressive rollouts. Under drift stress tests, our model's nested memory lowers MAE by 48-65% over a no-memory ablation. NeST-S6 also speeds full-grid reconstruction by 32 times and reduces MACs by 4.3 times compared to competitive per-pixel scanning models, while achieving 61% lower per-pixel RMSE.

AI.tldr: NeST-S6 is a convolutional selective state-space model with spatial PDE-awareness and nested memory learning that efficiently predicts cellular network traffic on spatiotemporal grids while handling spatial heterogeneity and reducing computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Traffic forecasting in cellular networks faces challenges: strong temporal dependencies, spatial heterogeneity across cells, scalability issues, and computational overhead. Traditional approaches either incur high training/maintenance costs (cell-specific models) or fail to capture spatial dynamics (global models), while recent attention/GNN-based architectures introduce excessive computational complexity for large-scale real-time applications.
AI.method: Proposes NeST-S6: a convolutional selective state-space model (SSM) with spatial PDE-aware core in a nested learning paradigm. Uses convolutional local spatial mixing feeding into spatial PDE-aware SSM core, with nested-learning long-term memory updated by learned optimizer when prediction errors indicate unmodeled dynamics. Works on spatiotemporal grid forecasting (2D lattice of traffic values) predicting next grid patch from previous patches.
AI.result: On Milan dataset at three resolutions (20², 50², 100²), NeST-S6 outperforms Mamba-family baseline in single-step and 6-step autoregressive rollouts. Under drift stress tests, nested memory reduces MAE by 48-65% over no-memory ablation. Achieves 32x speedup in full-grid reconstruction, 4.3x reduction in MACs compared to per-pixel scanning models, and 61% lower per-pixel RMSE.
AI.conclusion: NeST-S6 provides an efficient and effective solution for spatiotemporal grid forecasting in cellular networks, addressing key challenges of computational efficiency, spatial heterogeneity modeling, and long-term dependency capture through its convolutional SSM architecture with PDE-aware core and nested memory learning.

title: Semantic Invariance in Agentic AI
comment: Accepted for publication in 20th International Conference on Agents and Multi-Agent Systems: Technologies and Applications (AMSTA 2026), to appear in Springer Nature proceedings (KES Smart Innovation Systems and Technologies). The final authenticated version will be available online at Springer
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly serve as autonomous reasoning agents in decision support, scientific problem-solving, and multi-agent coordination systems. However, deploying LLM agents in consequential applications requires assurance that their reasoning remains stable under semantically equivalent input variations, a property we term semantic invariance.Standard benchmark evaluations, which assess accuracy on fixed, canonical problem formulations, fail to capture this critical reliability dimension. To address this shortcoming, in this paper we present a metamorphic testing framework for systematically assessing the robustness of LLM reasoning agents, applying eight semantic-preserving transformations (identity, paraphrase, fact reordering, expansion, contraction, academic context, business context, and contrastive formulation) across seven foundation models spanning four distinct architectural families: Hermes (70B, 405B), Qwen3 (30B-A3B, 235B-A22B), DeepSeek-R1, and gpt-oss (20B, 120B). Our evaluation encompasses 19 multi-step reasoning problems across eight scientific domains. The results reveal that model scale does not predict robustness: the smaller Qwen3-30B-A3B achieves the highest stability (79.6% invariant responses, semantic similarity 0.91), while larger models exhibit greater fragility.

AI.tldr: A metamorphic testing framework reveals that LLM reasoning agents often fail to produce semantically invariant responses under equivalent input variations, with model scale not correlating with robustness.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous reasoning agents in critical applications, but current benchmark evaluations fail to assess whether their reasoning remains stable under semantically equivalent input variations (semantic invariance), which is essential for reliability in consequential decision-making systems.
AI.method: Developed a metamorphic testing framework applying eight semantic-preserving transformations (identity, paraphrase, fact reordering, expansion, contraction, academic context, business context, contrastive formulation) across seven foundation models from four architectural families (Hermes, Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1, gpt-oss) on 19 multi-step reasoning problems across eight scientific domains.
AI.result: Model scale does not predict robustness - the smaller Qwen3-30B-A3B achieved the highest stability (79.6% invariant responses, semantic similarity 0.91), while larger models exhibited greater fragility, showing that semantic invariance is not correlated with model size.
AI.conclusion: Current benchmark evaluations are insufficient for assessing LLM reasoning reliability; semantic invariance testing reveals critical fragility issues that must be addressed for safe deployment of LLM agents in consequential applications, with model scale being a poor predictor of robustness.

title: Sinkhorn-Drifting Generative Models
summary: We establish a theoretical link between the recently proposed "drifting" generative dynamics and gradient flows induced by the Sinkhorn divergence. In a particle discretization, the drift field admits a cross-minus-self decomposition: an attractive term toward the target distribution and a repulsive/self-correction term toward the current model, both expressed via one-sided normalized Gibbs kernels. We show that Sinkhorn divergence yields an analogous cross-minus-self structure, but with each term defined by entropic optimal-transport couplings obtained through two-sided Sinkhorn scaling (i.e., enforcing both marginals). This provides a precise sense in which drifting acts as a surrogate for a Sinkhorn-divergence gradient flow, interpolating between one-sided normalization and full two-sided Sinkhorn scaling. Crucially, this connection resolves an identifiability gap in prior drifting formulations: leveraging the definiteness of the Sinkhorn divergence, we show that zero drift (equilibrium of the dynamics) implies that the model and target measures match. Experiments show that Sinkhorn drifting reduces sensitivity to kernel temperature and improves one-step generative quality, trading off additional training time for a more stable optimization, without altering the inference procedure used by drift methods. These theoretical gains translate to strong low-temperature improvements in practice: on FFHQ-ALAE at the lowest temperature setting we evaluate, Sinkhorn drifting reduces mean FID from 187.7 to 37.1 and mean latent EMD from 453.3 to 144.4, while on MNIST it preserves full class coverage across the temperature sweep. Project page: https://mint-vu.github.io/SinkhornDrifting/

AI.tldr: Sinkhorn drifting connects drifting generative dynamics to Sinkhorn divergence gradient flows, resolving identifiability issues and improving training stability and generative quality without changing inference procedures.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses limitations in prior drifting formulations, specifically an identifiability gap where zero drift doesn't guarantee model and target distribution matching. It aims to establish a theoretical link between drifting dynamics and Sinkhorn divergence gradient flows for more robust optimization.
AI.method: The authors show that Sinkhorn divergence yields a cross-minus-self structure similar to drifting dynamics, but with terms defined by entropic optimal-transport couplings obtained through two-sided Sinkhorn scaling. This connects drifting as a surrogate for Sinkhorn-divergence gradient flow, interpolating between one-sided normalization and full two-sided scaling.
AI.result: Sinkhorn drifting reduces sensitivity to kernel temperature, improves one-step generative quality, and provides more stable optimization. It achieves significant improvements in FID scores (from 187.7 to 37.1 on FFHQ-ALAE) and preserves full class coverage across temperature sweeps on MNIST.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes a theoretical link between drifting dynamics and Sinkhorn divergence gradient flows, resolving the identifiability gap in prior formulations. Sinkhorn drifting offers a more stable optimization approach that maintains inference efficiency while significantly improving generative quality and temperature robustness.

title: NeuroLoRA: Context-Aware Neuromodulation for Parameter-Efficient Multi-Task Adaptation
comment: work in progress
summary: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques, particularly Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), have become essential for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to downstream tasks. While the recent FlyLoRA framework successfully leverages bio-inspired sparse random projections to mitigate parameter interference, it relies on a static, magnitude-based routing mechanism that is agnostic to input context. In this paper, we propose NeuroLoRA, a novel Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) based LoRA framework inspired by biological neuromodulation -- the dynamic regulation of neuronal excitability based on context. NeuroLoRA retains the computational efficiency of frozen random projections while introducing a lightweight, learnable neuromodulation gate that contextually rescales the projection space prior to expert selection. We further propose a Contrastive Orthogonality Loss to explicitly enforce separation between expert subspaces, enhancing both task decoupling and continual learning capacity. Extensive experiments on MMLU, GSM8K, and ScienceQA demonstrate that NeuroLoRA consistently outperforms FlyLoRA and other strong baselines across single-task adaptation, multi-task model merging, and sequential continual learning scenarios, while maintaining comparable parameter efficiency.

AI.tldr: NeuroLoRA introduces a novel Mixture-of-Experts based LoRA framework inspired by biological neuromodulation, featuring context-aware gating and contrastive orthogonality loss for improved task adaptation and continual learning.
AI.motivation: Current PEFT methods like FlyLoRA use static, magnitude-based routing mechanisms that are agnostic to input context. The authors aim to develop a more dynamic, context-aware approach inspired by biological neuromodulation to better handle task adaptation and reduce parameter interference.
AI.method: NeuroLoRA combines frozen random projections (like FlyLoRA) with a lightweight, learnable neuromodulation gate that contextually rescales the projection space before expert selection. It also introduces a Contrastive Orthogonality Loss to enforce separation between expert subspaces.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on MMLU, GSM8K, and ScienceQA show NeuroLoRA consistently outperforms FlyLoRA and other baselines across single-task adaptation, multi-task model merging, and sequential continual learning, while maintaining comparable parameter efficiency.
AI.conclusion: NeuroLoRA successfully integrates biological neuromodulation principles into PEFT, creating a context-aware framework that improves task adaptation, reduces interference, and enhances continual learning capabilities while preserving computational efficiency.

title: SpectralGuard: Detecting Memory Collapse Attacks in State Space Models
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures. Code, dataset, and demo: https://github.com/DaviBonetto/spectralguard
summary: State Space Models (SSMs) such as Mamba achieve linear-time sequence processing through input-dependent recurrence, but this mechanism introduces a critical safety vulnerability. We show that the spectral radius rho(A-bar) of the discretized transition operator governs effective memory horizon: when an adversary drives rho toward zero through gradient-based Hidden State Poisoning, memory collapses from millions of tokens to mere dozens, silently destroying reasoning capacity without triggering output-level alarms. We prove an Evasion Existence Theorem showing that for any output-only defense, adversarial inputs exist that simultaneously induce spectral collapse and evade detection, then introduce SpectralGuard, a real-time monitor that tracks spectral stability across all model layers. SpectralGuard achieves F1=0.961 against non-adaptive attackers and retains F1=0.842 under the strongest adaptive setting, with sub-15ms per-token latency. Causal interventions and cross-architecture transfer to hybrid SSM-Attention systems confirm that spectral monitoring provides a principled, deployable safety layer for recurrent foundation models.

AI.tldr: Hidden State Poisoning attacks collapse memory in State Space Models like Mamba by reducing spectral radius, enabling evasion of output-based defenses; SpectralGuard monitors spectral stability for real-time protection.
AI.motivation: State Space Models achieve linear-time processing through input-dependent recurrence, but this mechanism creates a critical safety vulnerability where adversaries can collapse memory without detection, undermining reasoning capacity in deployed systems.
AI.method: The paper demonstrates gradient-based Hidden State Poisoning attacks that drive spectral radius toward zero, proves an Evasion Existence Theorem, and introduces SpectralGuard - a real-time monitor that tracks spectral stability across all model layers to detect these attacks.
AI.result: SpectralGuard achieves F1=0.961 against non-adaptive attackers and maintains F1=0.842 under strongest adaptive settings with sub-15ms per-token latency, with causal interventions and cross-architecture testing confirming effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Spectral monitoring provides a principled, deployable safety layer for recurrent foundation models by detecting memory collapse attacks that evade output-only defenses, addressing a fundamental vulnerability in SSM architectures.

title: Overcoming the Modality Gap in Context-Aided Forecasting
summary: Context-aided forecasting (CAF) holds promise for integrating domain knowledge and forward-looking information, enabling AI systems to surpass traditional statistical methods. However, recent empirical studies reveal a puzzling gap: multimodal models often fail to outperform their unimodal counterparts. We hypothesize that this underperformance stems from poor context quality in existing datasets, as verification is challenging. To address these limitations, we introduce a semi-synthetic data augmentation method that generates contexts both descriptive of temporal dynamics and verifiably complementary to numerical histories. This approach enables massive-scale dataset creation, resulting in CAF-7M, a corpus of 7 million context-augmented time series windows, including a rigorously verified test set. We demonstrate that semi-synthetic pre-training transfers effectively to real-world evaluation, and show clear evidence of context utilization. Our results suggest that dataset quality, rather than architectural limitations, has been the primary bottleneck in context-aided forecasting.

AI.tldr: A semi-synthetic data augmentation method creates CAF-7M dataset (7M context-augmented time series) to solve the multimodal forecasting gap, showing context quality, not architecture, is the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Context-aided forecasting (CAF) promises better performance than traditional methods by integrating domain knowledge, but empirical studies show multimodal models often fail to outperform unimodal ones. The authors hypothesize this gap comes from poor context quality in existing datasets where verification is challenging.
AI.method: Introduced a semi-synthetic data augmentation method that generates contexts both descriptive of temporal dynamics and verifiably complementary to numerical histories. This enables massive-scale dataset creation, resulting in CAF-7M corpus of 7 million context-augmented time series windows with a rigorously verified test set.
AI.result: Semi-synthetic pre-training transfers effectively to real-world evaluation, showing clear evidence of context utilization. The approach demonstrates that with high-quality context data, multimodal models can surpass unimodal counterparts.
AI.conclusion: Dataset quality, rather than architectural limitations, has been the primary bottleneck in context-aided forecasting. The semi-synthetic data generation approach provides a viable solution to create verifiable, high-quality context-augmented datasets.

title: Bases of Steerable Kernels for Equivariant CNNs: From 2D Rotations to the Lorentz Group
comment: 28 pages. Comments are welcome
summary: We present an alternative way of solving the steerable kernel constraint that appears in the design of steerable equivariant convolutional neural networks. We find explicit real and complex bases which are ready to use, for different symmetry groups and for feature maps of arbitrary tensor type. A major advantage of this method is that it bypasses the need to numerically or analytically compute Clebsch-Gordan coefficients and works directly with the representations of the input and output feature maps. The strategy is to find a basis of kernels that respect a simpler invariance condition at some point $x_0$, and then \textit{steer} it with the defining equation of steerability to move to some arbitrary point $x=g\cdot x_0$. This idea has already been mentioned in the literature before, but not advanced in depth and with some generality. Here we describe how it works with minimal technical tools to make it accessible for a general audience.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an alternative method for solving steerable kernel constraints in equivariant CNNs by finding explicit ready-to-use bases for different symmetry groups and tensor types, avoiding complex Clebsch-Gordan coefficient computations.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for designing steerable equivariant CNNs require numerically or analytically computing Clebsch-Gordan coefficients, which can be complex and computationally intensive. There's a need for more accessible and direct methods for constructing equivariant kernels.
AI.method: The method finds explicit real and complex bases for different symmetry groups and tensor types by first establishing a basis of kernels that satisfy invariance at a specific point x₀, then "steering" this basis to arbitrary points using the steerability equation (moving to x = g·x₀). This bypasses Clebsch-Gordan coefficient computations and works directly with input/output feature map representations.
AI.result: The approach yields ready-to-use explicit bases for steerable kernels applicable to various symmetry groups and tensor types. The method makes equivariant kernel construction more accessible by using minimal technical tools while maintaining generality.
AI.conclusion: This alternative approach to solving steerable kernel constraints provides a more accessible and direct method for designing steerable equivariant CNNs, with the advantage of working directly with representations without requiring complex Clebsch-Gordan coefficient computations.

title: Modal Logical Neural Networks for Financial AI
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, Accepted at ICLR 2026 FinAI
summary: The financial industry faces a critical dichotomy in AI adoption: deep learning often delivers strong empirical performance, while symbolic logic offers interpretability and rule adherence expected in regulated settings. We use Modal Logical Neural Networks (MLNNs) as a bridge between these worlds, integrating Kripke semantics into neural architectures to enable differentiable reasoning about necessity, possibility, time, and knowledge. We illustrate MLNNs as a differentiable ``Logic Layer'' for finance by mapping core components, Necessity Neurons ($\Box$) and Learnable Accessibility ($A_θ$), to regulatory guardrails, market stress testing, and collusion detection. Four case studies show how MLNN-style constraints can promote compliance in trading agents, help recover latent trust networks for market surveillance, encourage robustness under stress scenarios, and distinguish statistical belief from verified knowledge to help mitigate robo-advisory hallucinations.

AI.tldr: MLNNs bridge deep learning and symbolic logic by integrating Kripke semantics into neural networks for differentiable reasoning about necessity, possibility, time, and knowledge in financial applications.
AI.motivation: Financial industry faces AI adoption dichotomy: deep learning offers strong performance but lacks interpretability, while symbolic logic provides interpretability and rule adherence needed for regulated settings.
AI.method: Use Modal Logical Neural Networks (MLNNs) integrating Kripke semantics into neural architectures. Core components include Necessity Neurons (□) and Learnable Accessibility (Aθ). Applied as a differentiable "Logic Layer" for finance.
AI.result: Four case studies demonstrate MLNN-style constraints: 1) promote compliance in trading agents, 2) recover latent trust networks for market surveillance, 3) encourage robustness under stress scenarios, 4) distinguish statistical belief from verified knowledge to mitigate robo-advisory hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: MLNNs successfully bridge neural and symbolic approaches, providing a framework for building AI systems in finance that combine empirical performance with interpretability and regulatory compliance.

title: Probing Length Generalization in Mamba via Image Reconstruction
summary: Mamba has attracted widespread interest as a general-purpose sequence model due to its low computational complexity and competitive performance relative to transformers. However, its performance can degrade when inference sequence lengths exceed those seen during training. We study this phenomenon using a controlled vision task in which Mamba reconstructs images from sequences of image patches. By analyzing reconstructions at different stages of sequence processing, we reveal that Mamba qualitatively adapts its behavior to the distribution of sequence lengths encountered during training, resulting in strategies that fail to generalize beyond this range. To support our analysis, we introduce a length-adaptive variant of Mamba that improves performance across training sequence lengths. Our results provide an intuitive perspective on length generalization in Mamba and suggest directions for improving the architecture.

AI.tldr: Mamba's performance degrades with inference sequences longer than training lengths, shown through image reconstruction tasks. The paper introduces a length-adaptive Mamba variant to improve generalization.
AI.motivation: Despite Mamba's popularity as a sequence model with low computational complexity and competitive performance, it suffers from poor generalization when inference sequences exceed training lengths. The authors want to understand and address this limitation.
AI.method: Used controlled vision task where Mamba reconstructs images from sequences of image patches. Analyzed reconstructions at different stages of sequence processing. Introduced a length-adaptive variant of Mamba to improve performance across different sequence lengths.
AI.result: Mamba adapts its behavior to the distribution of sequence lengths encountered during training, developing strategies that fail to generalize beyond this range. The length-adaptive variant shows improved performance across training sequence lengths.
AI.conclusion: The study provides intuitive perspective on length generalization in Mamba and suggests architectural improvements. The length-adaptive approach offers a direction for enhancing Mamba's ability to handle sequences beyond training lengths.

title: Adaptive Conditional Forest Sampling for Spectral Risk Optimisation under Decision-Dependent Uncertainty
comment: 15 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables
summary: Minimising a spectral risk objective, defined as a convex combination of expected cost and Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR), is challenging when the uncertainty distribution is decision-dependent, making both surrogate modelling and simulation-based ranking sensitive to tail estimation error. We propose Adaptive Conditional Forest Sampling (ACFS), a four-phase simulation-optimisation framework that integrates Generalised Random Forests for decision-conditional distribution approximation, CEM-guided global exploration, rank-weighted focused augmentation, and surrogate-to-oracle two-stage reranking before multi-start gradient-based refinement. We evaluate ACFS on two structurally distinct data-generating processes: a decision-dependent Student-t copula and a Gaussian copula with log-normal marginals, across three penalty-weight configurations and 100 replications per setting. ACFS achieves the lowest median oracle spectral risk on the second benchmark in every configuration, with median gaps over GP-BO ranging from 6.0% to 20.0%. On the first benchmark, ACFS and GP-BO are statistically indistinguishable in median objective, but ACFS reduces cross-replication dispersion by approximately 1.8 to 1.9 times on the first benchmark and 1.7 to 2.0 times on the second, indicating materially improved run-to-run reliability. ACFS also outperforms CEM-SO, SGD-CVaR, and KDE-SO in nearly all settings, while ablation and sensitivity analyses support the contribution and robustness of the proposed design.

AI.tldr: ACFS is a four-phase simulation-optimization framework that integrates random forests and CEM to minimize spectral risk with decision-dependent uncertainty, outperforming other methods in reliability and performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Minimizing spectral risk (convex combination of expected cost and CVaR) is challenging when uncertainty distribution is decision-dependent, making both surrogate modeling and simulation-based ranking sensitive to tail behavior.
AI.method: Adaptive Conditional Forest Sampling (ACFS) - a four-phase framework: 1) Generalised Random Forests for decision-conditional distribution approximation, 2) CEM-guided global exploration, 3) rank-weighted focused augmentation, and 4) surrogate-to-oracle two-stage reranking before multi-start gradient-based refinement.
AI.result: ACFS achieves lowest median oracle spectral risk on second benchmark in every configuration (6.0-20.0% better than GP-BO). On first benchmark, statistically indistinguishable from GP-BO but reduces cross-replication dispersion by 1.8-1.9× on first benchmark and 1.7-2.0× on second, showing improved reliability. Outperforms CEM-SO, SGD-CVaR, and KDE-SO in nearly all settings.
AI.conclusion: ACFS effectively addresses decision-dependent spectral risk optimization with improved reliability and performance over existing methods, with ablation and sensitivity analyses supporting the design's contributions and robustness.

title: Byzantine-Robust Optimization under $(L_0, L_1)$-Smoothness
comment: 10 pages, 1 table, 4 figures, accepted to CPAL 2026
summary: We consider distributed optimization under Byzantine attacks in the presence of $(L_0,L_1)$-smoothness, a generalization of standard $L$-smoothness that captures functions with state-dependent gradient Lipschitz constants. We propose Byz-NSGDM, a normalized stochastic gradient descent method with momentum that achieves robustness against Byzantine workers while maintaining convergence guarantees. Our algorithm combines momentum normalization with Byzantine-robust aggregation enhanced by Nearest Neighbor Mixing (NNM) to handle both the challenges posed by $(L_0,L_1)$-smoothness and Byzantine adversaries. We prove that Byz-NSGDM achieves a convergence rate of $O(K^{-1/4})$ up to a Byzantine bias floor proportional to the robustness coefficient and gradient heterogeneity. Experimental validation on heterogeneous MNIST classification, synthetic $(L_0,L_1)$-smooth optimization, and character-level language modeling with a small GPT model demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach against various Byzantine attack strategies. An ablation study further shows that Byz-NSGDM is robust across a wide range of momentum and learning rate choices.

AI.tldr: Byz-NSGDM: A Byzantine-robust normalized stochastic gradient descent with momentum algorithm for distributed optimization under $(L_0,L_1)$-smoothness conditions
AI.motivation: Address distributed optimization under Byzantine attacks when dealing with $(L_0,L_1)$-smooth functions, which generalize standard smoothness to capture functions with state-dependent gradient Lipschitz constants
AI.method: Byz-NSGDM combines momentum normalization with Byzantine-robust aggregation enhanced by Nearest Neighbor Mixing (NNM) to handle both $(L_0,L_1)$-smoothness challenges and Byzantine adversaries
AI.result: Proves convergence rate of $O(K^{-1/4})$ up to a Byzantine bias floor proportional to robustness coefficient and gradient heterogeneity; experimental validation on MNIST classification, synthetic $(L_0,L_1)$-smooth optimization, and GPT language modeling shows effectiveness against various Byzantine attacks
AI.conclusion: Byz-NSGDM provides a robust solution for Byzantine-robust distributed optimization under generalized smoothness conditions, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness across diverse applications

title: When LLM Judge Scores Look Good but Best-of-N Decisions Fail
summary:
Large language models are often used as judges to score candidate responses, then validated with a single global metric such as correlation with reference labels. This can be misleading when the real deployment task is best-of-n selection within a prompt.
  In a 5,000-prompt best-of-4 benchmark from Chatbot Arena, a judge with moderate global correlation (r = 0.47) captures only 21.0% of the improvement that perfect selection would achieve over random choice. The gap arises because global agreement is driven largely by prompt-level baseline effects, while selection depends on within-prompt ranking: within-prompt correlation is only r_within = 0.27, and coarse pointwise scoring creates ties in 67% of pairwise comparisons.
  In a matched-pair best-of-2 audit, explicit pairwise judging recovers much of this lost signal, raising recovery from 21.1% to 61.2%. For judge-based selection, the relevant audit should report within-prompt signal, tie rates, and recovery/top-1 accuracy, not global agreement alone.

AI.tldr: LLM judges evaluated with global metrics like correlation can be misleading for best-of-n selection tasks, as global agreement is driven by prompt-level baseline effects rather than within-prompt ranking accuracy needed for selection.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of LLM judges using single global metrics (like correlation with reference labels) fails to capture their actual performance in real-world deployment scenarios where they're used for best-of-n selection within prompts.
AI.method: Conducted a 5,000-prompt best-of-4 benchmark from Chatbot Arena, analyzed the difference between global correlation and within-prompt correlation, examined tie rates in pairwise comparisons, and conducted a matched-pair best-of-2 audit comparing pointwise scoring vs explicit pairwise judging.
AI.result: A judge with moderate global correlation (r=0.47) captured only 21.0% of potential improvement over random selection, revealing a gap due to prompt-level baseline effects affecting global metrics. Within-prompt correlation was only r_within=0.27, with coarse pointwise scoring creating ties in 67% of pairwise comparisons. Explicit pairwise judging significantly improved recovery from 21.1% to 61.2%.
AI.conclusion: For judge-based selection tasks, evaluations should report within-prompt signal, tie rates, and recovery/top-1 accuracy rather than relying solely on global agreement metrics, as these better reflect actual deployment performance.

title: Learning Pore-scale Multiphase Flow from 4D Velocimetry
summary: Multiphase flow in porous media underpins subsurface energy and environmental technologies, including geological CO$_2$ storage and underground hydrogen storage, yet pore-scale dynamics in realistic three-dimensional materials remain difficult to characterize and predict. Here we introduce a multimodal learning framework that infers multiphase pore-scale flow directly from time-resolved four-dimensional (4D) micro-velocimetry measurements. The model couples a graph network simulator for Lagrangian tracer-particle motion with a 3D U-Net for voxelized interface evolution. The imaged pore geometry serves as a boundary constraint to the flow velocity and the multiphase interface predictions, which are coupled and updated iteratively at each time step. Trained autoregressively on experimental sequences in capillary-dominated conditions ($Ca\approx10^{-6}$), the learned surrogate captures transient, nonlocal flow perturbations and abrupt interface rearrangements (Haines jumps) over rollouts spanning seconds of physical time, while reducing hour-to-day--scale direct numerical simulations to seconds of inference. By providing rapid, experimentally informed predictions, the framework opens a route to ''digital experiments'' to replicate pore-scale physics observed in multiphase flow experiments, offering an efficient tool for exploring injection conditions and pore-geometry effects relevant to subsurface carbon and hydrogen storage.

AI.tldr: A multimodal learning framework that infers multiphase pore-scale flow from 4D micro-velocimetry measurements by coupling graph networks for particle motion with 3D U-Nets for interface evolution, enabling rapid digital experiments for subsurface energy applications.
AI.motivation: Multiphase flow in porous media is crucial for subsurface energy and environmental technologies like geological CO2 and hydrogen storage, but pore-scale dynamics in realistic 3D materials are difficult to characterize and predict using traditional methods.
AI.method: The framework combines a graph network simulator for Lagrangian tracer-particle motion with a 3D U-Net for voxelized interface evolution. Imaged pore geometry serves as boundary constraint, with flow velocity and multiphase interface predictions coupled and updated iteratively at each time step.
AI.result: The model captures transient, nonlocal flow perturbations and abrupt interface rearrangements (Haines jumps) over seconds of physical time while reducing hour-to-day-scale direct numerical simulations to seconds of inference. Trained autoregressively on experimental sequences in capillary-dominated conditions.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables rapid, experimentally informed predictions for "digital experiments" that replicate pore-scale physics, offering an efficient tool for exploring injection conditions and pore-geometry effects relevant to subsurface carbon and hydrogen storage applications.

title: TERMINATOR: Learning Optimal Exit Points for Early Stopping in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
comment: 35 pages, 31 figures
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve impressive performance on complex reasoning tasks via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, which enables them to generate intermediate thinking tokens before arriving at the final answer. However, LRMs often suffer from significant overthinking, spending excessive compute time even after the answer is generated early on. Prior work has identified the existence of an optimal reasoning length such that truncating reasoning at this point significantly shortens CoT outputs with virtually no change in performance. However, determining optimal CoT lengths for practical datasets is highly non-trivial as they are fully task and model-dependent. In this paper, we precisely address this and design TERMINATOR, an early-exit strategy for LRMs at inference to mitigate overthinking. The central idea underpinning TERMINATOR is that the first arrival of an LRM's final answer is often predictable, and we leverage these first answer positions to create a novel dataset of optimal reasoning lengths to train TERMINATOR. Powered by this approach, TERMINATOR achieves significant reductions in CoT lengths of 14%-55% on average across four challenging practical datasets: MATH-500, AIME 2025, HumanEval, and GPQA, whilst outperforming current state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: TERMINATOR is an early-exit strategy that predicts optimal reasoning lengths for Large Reasoning Models to prevent overthinking, achieving 14%-55% reduction in Chain-of-Thought lengths while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models suffer from significant overthinking, spending excessive compute time even after generating correct answers early in their reasoning process, which wastes computational resources.
AI.method: TERMINATOR leverages the predictability of when LRMs first arrive at final answers to create a dataset of optimal reasoning lengths, then trains an early-exit strategy to truncate reasoning at these optimal points.
AI.result: TERMINATOR achieves average CoT length reductions of 14%-55% across four challenging datasets (MATH-500, AIME 2025, HumanEval, GPQA) while outperforming current state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the overthinking problem in LRMs by introducing TERMINATOR, an effective early-exit strategy that significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining reasoning performance.

title: Curriculum Sampling: A Two-Phase Curriculum for Efficient Training of Flow Matching
summary: Timestep sampling $p(t)$ is a central design choice in Flow Matching models, yet common practice increasingly favors static middle-biased distributions (e.g., Logit-Normal). We show that this choice induces a speed--quality trade-off: middle-biased sampling accelerates early convergence but yields worse asymptotic fidelity than Uniform sampling. By analyzing per-timestep training losses, we identify a U-shaped difficulty profile with persistent errors near the boundary regimes, implying that under-sampling the endpoints leaves fine details unresolved. Guided by this insight, we propose \textbf{Curriculum Sampling}, a two-phase schedule that begins with middle-biased sampling for rapid structure learning and then switches to Uniform sampling for boundary refinement. On CIFAR-10, Curriculum Sampling improves the best FID from $3.85$ (Uniform) to $3.22$ while reaching peak performance at $100$k rather than $150$k training steps. Our results highlight that timestep sampling should be treated as an evolving curriculum rather than a fixed hyperparameter.

AI.tldr: Curriculum Sampling improves Flow Matching models by using middle-biased sampling early for fast structure learning, then switching to uniform sampling later for boundary refinement, achieving better FID with fewer training steps.
AI.motivation: Common practice in Flow Matching models uses static middle-biased timestep sampling (like Logit-Normal), but this creates a speed-quality trade-off: it accelerates early convergence but yields worse asymptotic fidelity than uniform sampling. The authors identify persistent errors near boundary regimes that remain unresolved with middle-biased sampling.
AI.method: The authors analyze per-timestep training losses and discover a U-shaped difficulty profile, showing that errors persist near boundaries. They propose Curriculum Sampling, a two-phase schedule that starts with middle-biased sampling for rapid structure learning, then switches to uniform sampling specifically for boundary refinement to resolve fine details.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10, Curriculum Sampling improves the best FID from 3.85 (with uniform sampling) to 3.22, while reaching peak performance at only 100k training steps instead of 150k. This demonstrates both improved quality and faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: Timestep sampling should be treated as an evolving curriculum rather than a fixed hyperparameter. The dynamic approach of Curriculum Sampling leverages the strengths of different sampling strategies at different training phases, overcoming the limitations of static sampling distributions.

title: Embedded Quantum Machine Learning in Embedded Systems: Feasibility, Hybrid Architectures, and Quantum Co-Processors
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure, 5th International Conference Computing, Mathematics & Engineering Technologies (iCoMET 2026)
summary: Embedded quantum machine learning (EQML) seeks to bring quantum machine learning (QML) capabilities to resource-constrained edge platforms such as IoT nodes, wearables, drones, and cyber-physical controllers. In 2026, EQML is technically feasible only in limited and highly experimental forms: (i) hybrid workflows where an embedded device performs sensing and classical processing while offloading a narrowly scoped quantum subroutine to a remote quantum processing unit (QPU) or nearby quantum appliance, and (ii) early-stage "embedded QPU" concepts in which a compact quantum co-processor is integrated with classical control hardware. A practical bridge is quantum-inspired machine learning and optimisation on classical embedded processors and FPGAs. This paper analyses feasibility from a circuits-and-systems perspective aligned with the academic community, formalises two implementation pathways, identifies the dominant barriers (latency, data encoding overhead, NISQ noise, tooling mismatch, and energy), and maps them to concrete engineering directions in interface design, control electronics, power management, verification, and security. We also argue that responsible deployment requires adversarial evaluation and governance practices that are increasingly necessary for edge AI systems.

AI.tldr: EQML brings quantum ML to edge devices; currently feasible only through hybrid workflows (offloading to remote QPUs) or embedded QPU concepts; practical bridge is quantum-inspired classical processing. Paper analyzes feasibility from circuits perspective, formalizes pathways, identifies barriers, and maps engineering solutions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to bring quantum machine learning capabilities to resource-constrained edge platforms like IoT nodes, wearables, drones, and cyber-physical controllers, which currently lack access to quantum computing benefits.
AI.method: The paper analyzes feasibility from a circuits-and-systems perspective, formalizes two implementation pathways: 1) hybrid workflows with offloading to remote QPUs, and 2) early-stage embedded QPU concepts. It also considers quantum-inspired ML on classical hardware as a practical bridge.
AI.result: The analysis identifies dominant barriers including latency, data encoding overhead, NISQ noise, tooling mismatch, and energy constraints. It maps these to concrete engineering directions in interface design, control electronics, power management, verification, and security.
AI.conclusion: EQML is currently only feasible in limited experimental forms, requiring hybrid architectures. Responsible deployment requires adversarial evaluation and governance practices, similar to those needed for edge AI systems, to address security and ethical concerns.

title: CALF: Communication-Aware Learning Framework for Distributed Reinforcement Learning
summary: Distributed reinforcement learning policies face network delays, jitter, and packet loss when deployed across edge devices and cloud servers. Standard RL training assumes zero-latency interaction, causing severe performance degradation under realistic network conditions. We introduce CALF (Communication-Aware Learning Framework), which trains policies under realistic network models during simulation. Systematic experiments demonstrate that network-aware training substantially reduces deployment performance gaps compared to network-agnostic baselines. Distributed policy deployments across heterogeneous hardware validate that explicitly modelling communication constraints during training enables robust real-world execution. These findings establish network conditions as a major axis of sim-to-real transfer for Wi-Fi-like distributed deployments, complementing physics and visual domain randomisation.

AI.tldr: CALF trains RL policies with simulated network delays to improve real-world distributed deployment performance, bridging the sim-to-real gap for network conditions.
AI.motivation: Distributed RL policies suffer performance degradation when deployed across edge-cloud systems due to network delays, jitter, and packet loss, which are ignored in standard zero-latency training assumptions.
AI.method: Introduces CALF (Communication-Aware Learning Framework) that trains reinforcement learning policies under realistic network models during simulation, explicitly modeling communication constraints.
AI.result: Network-aware training substantially reduces deployment performance gaps compared to network-agnostic baselines, enabling robust execution across heterogeneous hardware in real-world deployments.
AI.conclusion: Network conditions represent a critical dimension of sim-to-real transfer for distributed RL deployments, complementing existing domain randomization techniques for physics and visuals.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion LLMs with Entropy-Guided Step Selection and Stepwise Advantages
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been effective for post-training autoregressive (AR) language models, but extending these methods to diffusion language models (DLMs) is challenging due to intractable sequence-level likelihoods. Existing approaches therefore rely on surrogate likelihoods or heuristic approximations, which can introduce bias and obscure the sequential structure of denoising. We formulate diffusion-based sequence generation as a finite-horizon Markov decision process over the denoising trajectory and derive an exact, unbiased policy gradient that decomposes over denoising steps and is expressed in terms of intermediate advantages, without requiring explicit evaluation of the sequence likelihood. To obtain a practical and compute-efficient estimator, we (i) select denoising steps for policy updates via an entropy-guided approximation bound, and (ii) estimate intermediate advantages using a one-step denoising reward naturally provided by the diffusion model, avoiding costly multi-step rollouts. Experiments on coding and logical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art results, with strong competitive performance on mathematical reasoning, outperforming existing RL post-training approaches for DLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/vishnutez/egspo-dllm-rl.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an exact, unbiased policy gradient method for reinforcement learning post-training of diffusion language models by formulating denoising as a finite-horizon Markov decision process, avoiding sequence-level likelihood evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods work well for autoregressive language models but are challenging to extend to diffusion language models due to intractable sequence-level likelihoods. Current approaches rely on surrogate likelihoods or heuristic approximations that introduce bias and obscure the sequential structure of denoising.
AI.method: 1) Formulate diffusion-based sequence generation as a finite-horizon Markov decision process over the denoising trajectory. 2) Derive an exact, unbiased policy gradient that decomposes over denoising steps using intermediate advantages. 3) Use entropy-guided approximation bound to select denoising steps for policy updates. 4) Estimate intermediate advantages using one-step denoising rewards from the diffusion model to avoid costly multi-step rollouts.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art results on coding and logical reasoning benchmarks, with strong competitive performance on mathematical reasoning. It outperforms existing RL post-training approaches for diffusion language models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an exact, unbiased alternative to existing heuristic approaches for RL post-training of diffusion language models, enabling efficient policy optimization without requiring sequence likelihood evaluation while maintaining the sequential structure of denoising.

title: A Reduction Algorithm for Markovian Contextual Linear Bandits
summary: Recent work shows that when contexts are drawn i.i.d., linear contextual bandits can be reduced to single-context linear bandits. This ``contexts are cheap" perspective is highly advantageous, as it allows for sharper finite-time analyses and leverages mature techniques from the linear bandit literature, such as those for misspecification and adversarial corruption. Motivated by applications with temporally correlated availability, we extend this perspective to Markovian contextual linear bandits, where the action set evolves via an exogenous Markov chain. Our main contribution is a reduction that applies under uniform geometric ergodicity. We construct a stationary surrogate action set to solve the problem using a standard linear bandit oracle, employing a delayed-update scheme to control the bias induced by the nonstationary conditional context distributions. We further provide a phased algorithm for unknown transition distributions that learns the surrogate mapping online. In both settings, we obtain a high-probability worst-case regret bound matching that of the underlying linear bandit oracle, with only lower-order dependence on the mixing time.

AI.tldr: Reduces Markovian contextual linear bandits to linear bandits via stationary surrogate action sets, enabling application of existing linear bandit techniques with only mixing-time overhead.
AI.motivation: Extend the "contexts are cheap" reduction approach from i.i.d. contexts to Markovian contexts, motivated by applications with temporally correlated availability where action sets evolve via exogenous Markov chains.
AI.method: Construct stationary surrogate action sets using reduction under uniform geometric ergodicity. Employ delayed-update scheme to control bias from nonstationary conditional context distributions. Also provide phased algorithm for unknown transition distributions that learns surrogate mapping online.
AI.result: Obtain high-probability worst-case regret bound matching that of underlying linear bandit oracle, with only lower-order dependence on mixing time rather than linear dependence.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends the reduction framework to Markovian contexts, making existing linear bandit techniques (including those for misspecification and adversarial corruption) applicable to temporally correlated settings with minimal performance degradation.

title: CA-HFP: Curvature-Aware Heterogeneous Federated Pruning with Model Reconstruction
summary: Federated learning on heterogeneous edge devices requires personalized compression while preserving aggregation compatibility and stable convergence. We present Curvature-Aware Heterogeneous Federated Pruning (CA-HFP), a practical framework that enables each client perform structured, device-specific pruning guided by a curvature-informed significance score, and subsequently maps its compact submodel back into a common global parameter space via a lightweight reconstruction. We derive a convergence bound for federated optimization with multiple local SGD steps that explicitly accounts for local computation, data heterogeneity, and pruning-induced perturbations; from which a principled loss-based pruning criterion is derived. Extensive experiments on FMNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 using VGG and ResNet architectures under varying degrees of data heterogeneity demonstrate that CA-HFP preserves model accuracy while significantly reducing per-client computation and communication costs, outperforming standard federated training and existing pruning-based baselines.

AI.tldr: CA-HFP enables personalized compression for heterogeneous edge devices in federated learning by performing device-specific pruning with curvature guidance and mapping to a global parameter space, achieving accuracy preservation while reducing computation/communication costs.
AI.motivation: Federated learning on heterogeneous edge devices requires personalized compression while maintaining aggregation compatibility and stable convergence - current approaches don't adequately address device heterogeneity with pruning.
AI.method: Curvature-Aware Heterogeneous Federated Pruning (CA-HFP): Each client performs structured, device-specific pruning using a curvature-informed significance score, then maps the compact submodel back to common global parameter space via lightweight reconstruction. Convergence bound analysis accounts for local computation, data heterogeneity, and pruning perturbations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on FMNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 with VGG/ResNet architectures under varying data heterogeneity show CA-HFP preserves model accuracy while significantly reducing per-client computation and communication costs, outperforming standard federated training and existing pruning-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: CA-HFP provides a practical framework for personalized compression in heterogeneous federated learning that maintains aggregation compatibility and stable convergence while achieving efficiency gains, addressing key challenges in edge device deployments.

title: Swap-guided Preference Learning for Personalized Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
comment: ICLR 2026
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a widely used approach to align large-scale AI systems with human values. However, RLHF typically assumes a single, universal reward, which overlooks diverse preferences and limits personalization. Variational Preference Learning (VPL) seeks to address this by introducing user-specific latent variables. Despite its promise, we found that VPL suffers from posterior collapse. While this phenomenon is well known in VAEs, it has not previously been identified in preference learning frameworks. Under sparse preference data and with overly expressive decoders, VPL may cause latent variables to be ignored, reverting to a single-reward model. To overcome this limitation, we propose Swap-guided Preference Learning (SPL). The key idea is to construct fictitious swap annotators and use the mirroring property of their preferences to guide the encoder. SPL introduces three components: (1) swap-guided base regularization, (2) Preferential Inverse Autoregressive Flow (P-IAF), and (3) adaptive latent conditioning. Experiments show that SPL mitigates collapse, enriches user-specific latents, and improves preference prediction. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/cobang0111/SPL

AI.tldr: Swap-guided Preference Learning (SPL) addresses posterior collapse in Variational Preference Learning (VPL) for personalized RLHF by using swap annotators and three novel components to preserve user-specific latent variables.
AI.motivation: RLHF assumes a single universal reward, overlooking diverse human preferences and limiting personalization. VPL attempts to address this with user-specific latents but suffers from posterior collapse where latent variables are ignored, reverting to single-reward models.
AI.method: SPL introduces: 1) swap-guided base regularization using fictitious swap annotators and mirroring preferences to guide the encoder, 2) Preferential Inverse Autoregressive Flow (P-IAF) for expressive variational distributions, and 3) adaptive latent conditioning to preserve user-specific information.
AI.result: SPL successfully mitigates posterior collapse, enriches user-specific latent representations, and improves preference prediction performance compared to VPL baselines.
AI.conclusion: SPL effectively addresses the posterior collapse problem in variational preference learning, enabling better personalization in RLHF by preserving diverse user preferences through its novel swap-guided approach and architectural improvements.

title: As Language Models Scale, Low-order Linear Depth Dynamics Emerge
summary: Large language models are often viewed as high-dimensional nonlinear systems and treated as black boxes. Here, we show that transformer depth dynamics admit accurate low-order linear surrogates within context. Across tasks including toxicity, irony, hate speech and sentiment, a 32-dimensional linear surrogate reproduces the layerwise sensitivity profile of GPT-2-large with near-perfect agreement, capturing how the final output shifts under additive injections at each layer. We then uncover a surprising scaling principle: for a fixed-order linear surrogate, agreement with the full model improves monotonically with model size across the GPT-2 family. This linear surrogate also enables principled multi-layer interventions that require less energy than standard heuristic schedules when applied to the full model. Together, our results reveal that as language models scale, low-order linear depth dynamics emerge within contexts, offering a systems-theoretic foundation for analyzing and controlling them.

AI.tldr: Large language models like GPT-2 exhibit emergent low-order linear dynamics in their transformer depth, enabling accurate linear surrogates that capture layerwise behavior and improve with model scaling.
AI.motivation: LLMs are typically treated as black-box nonlinear systems, but understanding their internal dynamics could enable better analysis and control. The paper aims to uncover simpler structures within complex transformer models.
AI.method: Developed low-order linear surrogates (32-dimensional) to approximate transformer depth dynamics. Tested across multiple tasks (toxicity, irony, hate speech, sentiment) on GPT-2 models. Studied scaling behavior across GPT-2 family sizes.
AI.result: The linear surrogate reproduces GPT-2-large's layerwise sensitivity with near-perfect agreement. Found a scaling principle: linear surrogate accuracy improves monotonically with model size. Enables energy-efficient multi-layer interventions compared to heuristic approaches.
AI.conclusion: As LLMs scale, low-order linear depth dynamics emerge within contexts, providing a systems-theoretic foundation for analyzing and controlling language models rather than treating them as black boxes.

title: Optimize Wider, Not Deeper: Consensus Aggregation for Policy Optimization
summary: Proximal policy optimization (PPO) approximates the trust region update using multiple epochs of clipped SGD. Each epoch may drift further from the natural gradient direction, creating path-dependent noise. To understand this drift, we can use Fisher information geometry to decompose policy updates into signal (the natural gradient projection) and waste (the Fisher-orthogonal residual that consumes trust region budget without first-order surrogate improvement). Empirically, signal saturates but waste grows with additional epochs, creating an optimization-depth dilemma. We propose Consensus Aggregation for Policy Optimization (CAPO), which redirects compute from depth to width: $K$ PPO replicates are optimized on the same batch, differing only in minibatch shuffling order, and then aggregated into a consensus. We study aggregation in two spaces: Euclidean parameter space, and the natural parameter space of the policy distribution via the logarithmic opinion pool. In natural parameter space, the consensus provably achieves higher KL-penalized surrogate and tighter trust region compliance than the mean expert; parameter averaging inherits these guarantees approximately. On continuous control tasks, CAPO outperforms PPO and compute-matched deeper baselines under fixed sample budgets by up to 8.6x. CAPO demonstrates that policy optimization can be improved by optimizing wider, rather than deeper, without additional environment interactions.

AI.tldr: CAPO improves PPO by running multiple parallel PPO replicas with different minibatch orders and aggregating their updates, achieving better performance by optimizing wider rather than deeper without extra environment interactions.
AI.motivation: PPO suffers from path-dependent noise where additional optimization epochs drift from the natural gradient direction, causing wasted trust region budget. The paper identifies that signal saturates while waste grows with more epochs, creating an "optimization-depth dilemma."
AI.method: CAPO runs K PPO replicas in parallel on the same batch, differing only in minibatch shuffling order, then aggregates their updates. Two aggregation spaces are studied: Euclidean parameter space and natural parameter space (via logarithmic opinion pool), with the latter provably achieving better theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: CAPO outperforms standard PPO and compute-matched deeper baselines by up to 8.6x on continuous control tasks under fixed sample budgets. The consensus aggregation in natural parameter space achieves higher KL-penalized surrogate and tighter trust region compliance.
AI.conclusion: Policy optimization can be significantly improved by redirecting compute from optimization depth to width through parallel replica training and consensus aggregation, without requiring additional environment interactions.

title: Deep Distance Measurement Method for Unsupervised Multivariate Time Series Similarity Retrieval
comment: Workshop of Artificial Intelligence for Time Series Analysis (AI4TS): Theory, Algorithms, and Applications at 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM), 2025
summary: We propose the Deep Distance Measurement Method (DDMM) to improve retrieval accuracy in unsupervised multivariate time series similarity retrieval. DDMM enables learning of minute differences within states in the entire time series and thereby recognition of minute differences between states, which are of interest to users in industrial plants. To achieve this, DDMM uses a learning algorithm that assigns a weight to each pair of an anchor and a positive sample, arbitrarily sampled from the entire time series, based on the Euclidean distance within the pair and learns the differences within the pairs weighted by the weights. This algorithm allows both learning minute differences within states and sampling pairs from the entire time series. Our empirical studies showed that DDMM significantly outperformed state-of-the-art time series representation learning methods on the Pulp-and-paper mill dataset and demonstrated the effectiveness of DDMM in industrial plants. Furthermore, we showed that accuracy can be further improved by linking DDMM with existing feature extraction methods through experiments with the combined model.

AI.tldr: DDMM is a novel unsupervised method for multivariate time series similarity retrieval that learns minute differences within states using weighted anchor-positive pair sampling and distance-based weighting, significantly outperforming SOTA methods on industrial datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for time series similarity retrieval struggle to capture minute differences within states that are critical for industrial plant monitoring and analysis. Users in industrial settings need to recognize subtle differences between operational states for effective plant management.
AI.method: DDMM uses an anchor-positive pair sampling strategy from entire time series with a learning algorithm that assigns weights to each pair based on Euclidean distance. The method learns differences within pairs weighted by these distance-based weights, enabling both learning of minute differences and sampling from entire time series.
AI.result: DDMM significantly outperformed state-of-the-art time series representation learning methods on the Pulp-and-paper mill dataset. Additionally, combining DDMM with existing feature extraction methods further improved accuracy in experiments.
AI.conclusion: DDMM is an effective unsupervised method for multivariate time series similarity retrieval in industrial plants, capable of learning minute differences within states and outperforming existing methods, with potential for further enhancement through integration with feature extraction techniques.

title: FastDSAC: Unlocking the Potential of Maximum Entropy RL in High-Dimensional Humanoid Control
summary: Scaling Maximum Entropy Reinforcement Learning (RL) to high-dimensional humanoid control remains a formidable challenge, as the ``curse of dimensionality'' induces severe exploration inefficiency and training instability in expansive action spaces. Consequently, recent high-throughput paradigms have largely converged on deterministic policy gradients combined with massive parallel simulation. We challenge this compromise with FastDSAC, a framework that effectively unlocks the potential of maximum entropy stochastic policies for complex continuous control. We introduce Dimension-wise Entropy Modulation (DEM) to dynamically redistribute the exploration budget and enforce diversity, alongside a continuous distributional critic tailored to ensure value fidelity and mitigate high-dimensional value overestimation.
